subject: biology
ncdc_competency: The learner evaluates the regulation and maintenance of optimal internal environment in living organisms by analysing how organ systems generate and eliminate metabolic wastes and maintain the functioning of cells, to make life choices that promote wellness.

content.body: Homeostasis is the maintenance of a constant internal environment despite changes in the external environment. It is essential for survival because cells can only function optimally within narrow ranges of temperature, pH, glucose concentration, water potential, and ion concentration.
Why is a Stable Internal Environment Important?

items: Enzyme activity: enzymes have an optimum temperature and pH. Deviations denature enzymes or slow reactions — disrupting metabolism.
Osmosis: cells gain or lose water by osmosis if the water potential of surrounding fluid changes. Cells shrink (crenation) in hypertonic solutions or burst (lysis) in hypotonic solutions.
Nerve function: the correct balance of Na⁺ and K⁺ ions is critical for nerve impulse transmission — disrupted by changes in blood ion concentration.
Blood glucose: too low (hypoglycaemia) → brain cells starved of energy → coma. Too high (hyperglycaemia) → osmotic damage to blood vessel walls, kidney damage, blindness.

content.body: Components of a Homeostatic Control System

items: Receptor (sensor): detects deviation from the set point (normal value). Example: thermoreceptors in the hypothalamus detect body temperature changes.
Control centre (coordinator): receives information from receptor and compares it to the set point. Determines the appropriate response. Example: hypothalamus in the brain.
Effector: carries out the corrective response. Example: sweat glands, muscles, blood vessels.
Set point: the normal value maintained by the system. Example: body temperature ~37°C, blood glucose ~5 mmol/L.
Feedback loop: information from the effector's response is fed back to the receptor/control centre.

content.body: In negative feedback, a deviation from the set point triggers a corrective response that reverses the deviation — bringing conditions back to the set point. This is the mechanism used in virtually all homeostatic systems.

content.heading: How negative feedback works:
items: Normal set point maintained.
Stimulus causes a deviation (either above or below set point).
Receptor detects the change.
Control centre initiates a corrective response via effectors.
Effectors act to reverse the change.
Conditions return to set point.
Receptor detects return to set point → response is switched off.
The system oscillates slightly around the set point rather than maintaining it perfectly.

content.body: Endotherms (mammals and birds) maintain a constant core body temperature using internal metabolic heat. Humans maintain ~37°C. The hypothalamus acts as the thermostat.

content.heading: Responses to overheating (body temperature above 37°C):
items: Sweating: sweat glands secrete sweat onto skin surface. Water evaporates, absorbing latent heat from the skin — cooling the body. Controlled by sympathetic nerves.

Vasodilation: arterioles near the skin surface dilate — more blood flows to skin capillaries. Heat is lost from blood to the environment by radiation, conduction, and convection. Skin appears flushed (red).
Hair/fur lying flat (in other mammals): reduces insulating air layer — increases heat loss.
Reduced metabolic rate: liver produces less heat.
Behavioural responses: seeking shade, removing clothing.

content.heading: Responses to cooling (body temperature below 37°C):
items: Shivering: skeletal muscles contract rapidly and involuntarily. Muscle contraction generates heat from cellular respiration. ~70–80% of energy from muscle respiration is released as heat.
Vasoconstriction: arterioles near the skin surface constrict — less blood flows to skin. Blood is redirected to core organs — reducing heat loss from skin. Skin appears pale.
Piloerection (goosebumps): arrector pili muscles contract, raising hairs. Traps a layer of still, insulating air next to skin (more effective in animals with thick fur).

Increased metabolic rate: thyroid hormones (thyroxine) and adrenaline increase metabolic rate — more heat generated. Brown adipose tissue in infants generates heat by uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation (heat generated instead of ATP).
Behavioural responses: huddling, wearing clothing, seeking warmth.

questions.question: A student runs a marathon on a hot day. Their body temperature begins to rise above 37°C. Which combination of responses would help return temperature to the set point?
options: Shivering and vasoconstriction
Sweating and vasodilation of skin arterioles
Piloerection and reduced metabolic rate
Sweating and vasoconstriction
questions.answer: Sweating and vasodilation of skin arterioles
questions.explanation: When body temperature rises above 37°C, sweating removes heat through evaporation and vasodilation brings more blood to the skin surface for heat loss by radiation and convection. Vasoconstriction and shivering are responses to cold — the opposite situation.

questions.question: The hypothalamus monitors blood temperature directly. If blood temperature drops, it sends nerve impulses causing vasoconstriction. This is an example of:
options: Positive feedback
Negative feedback
A reflex arc
Allosteric regulation
questions.explanation: Vasoconstriction reduces heat loss, counteracting the temperature drop and returning body temperature toward 37°C. This reversal of the change is the hallmark of negative feedback — the response opposes the original deviation.

questions.question: A patient with type 2 diabetes has persistently high blood glucose (hyperglycaemia). One long-term consequence is kidney damage. This occurs because:
options: Glucose is toxic to the immune system
High glucose creates high osmotic pressure in kidney blood vessels, damaging the delicate capillary walls
Glucose replaces ADH in the kidney tubules
High glucose reduces insulin secretion permanently
questions.answer: High glucose creates high osmotic pressure in kidney blood vessels, damaging the delicate capillary walls
questions.explanation: Persistent hyperglycaemia causes osmotic stress on the walls of small blood vessels (microangiopathy), particularly in the glomeruli of the kidney. Over years, this damages the filtration membranes, causing protein to leak into urine (proteinuria) and eventually kidney failure (diabetic nephropathy).

content.body: Blood glucose concentration is maintained at approximately 5 mmol/L (90 mg/100 mL) by the pancreatic hormones insulin and glucagon, in a classic negative feedback system. The islets of Langerhans in the pancreas contain alpha cells (producing glucagon) and beta cells (producing insulin).

items: Beta cells in pancreatic islets detect rise in blood glucose.
Beta cells secrete insulin into the bloodstream.
Insulin binds to receptor proteins on liver cells, muscle cells, and adipose (fat) cells.

Effects of insulin: (1) increases permeability of cell membranes to glucose — more glucose enters cells; (2) activates enzymes that convert glucose to glycogen (glycogenesis) in liver and muscle; (3) promotes glucose oxidation in cells; (4) promotes fat synthesis (lipogenesis) from excess glucose; (5) inhibits glycogen breakdown.
Blood glucose returns to ~5 mmol/L.
Beta cells detect return to set point → stop secreting insulin.

content.body: Between meals or during exercise (blood glucose falls):

items: Alpha cells in pancreatic islets detect fall in blood glucose.
Alpha cells secrete glucagon into the bloodstream.
Glucagon binds to receptor proteins mainly on liver cells.

Effects of glucagon: (1) activates enzymes that break down glycogen to glucose (glycogenolysis) in the liver; (2) promotes gluconeogenesis — synthesis of new glucose from non-carbohydrate sources (amino acids, glycerol) in the liver; (3) promotes fat breakdown (lipolysis) — fatty acids used for respiration instead of glucose.
Blood glucose returns to ~5 mmol/L.
Alpha cells detect return to set point → stop secreting glucagon.

content.heading: Type 1 Diabetes (Insulin-Dependent):
items: Cause: autoimmune destruction of beta cells — the immune system attacks and destroys its own insulin-producing cells.
Result: little or no insulin produced. Blood glucose rises dangerously (hyperglycaemia) after meals.
Glucose spills into urine (glucosuria) — draws water with it by osmosis → excessive urination (polyuria) and thirst.
Without insulin, cells cannot take up glucose → cells break down fats and proteins for energy → produces ketone bodies → ketoacidosis (dangerously low blood pH) → diabetic coma.
Treatment: insulin injections (cannot be taken orally — protein would be digested). Diet management. Insulin pumps.

content.heading: Type 2 Diabetes (Insulin-Resistant):
items: Cause: body cells become less responsive to insulin (insulin resistance). Associated with obesity, physical inactivity, poor diet.
Result: blood glucose remains elevated despite insulin being present — insulin receptors on cells do not respond effectively.
Uganda: type 2 diabetes is rapidly increasing due to urbanisation, changing diets (processed foods, sugary drinks), and reduced physical activity.
Treatment: lifestyle changes (diet, exercise), oral medications (e.g. metformin), insulin injections in advanced cases.
Long-term complications of poorly controlled diabetes: retinopathy (blindness), neuropathy (nerve damage), nephropathy (kidney failure), cardiovascular disease, diabetic foot ulcers.

questions.question: After eating a large meal of matoke and beans, blood glucose rises. Which sequence correctly describes the homeostatic response?
options: Alpha cells secrete glucagon → liver breaks down glycogen
Beta cells secrete insulin → liver and muscle cells convert glucose to glycogen
Hypothalamus secretes ADH → kidneys reabsorb glucose
Beta cells secrete glucagon → fat cells absorb glucose
questions.answer: Beta cells secrete insulin → liver and muscle cells convert glucose to glycogen
questions.explanation: Rising blood glucose is detected by beta cells in the islets of Langerhans. They secrete insulin, which stimulates glucose uptake by cells and promotes glycogenesis (glucose → glycogen) in liver and muscle, lowering blood glucose back to the set point.

questions.question: A marathon runner's blood glucose begins to fall during the race. Glucagon is released. Which process does glucagon stimulate in the liver?
options: Glycogenesis — converting glucose to glycogen
Glycogenolysis — breaking down glycogen to release glucose
Insulin secretion to compensate
Increased glucose absorption from the intestine
questions.answer: Glycogenolysis — breaking down glycogen to release glucose
questions.explanation: Glucagon activates phosphorylase enzyme in the liver, which breaks glycogen down to glucose-1-phosphate, then glucose, which is released into the blood. Glucagon also stimulates gluconeogenesis. Together these raise blood glucose back to the set point.

questions.question: A 12-year-old boy is diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes. He cannot take insulin tablets — he must inject it. The reason he cannot take insulin orally is:
options: Insulin is too large to be absorbed through the stomach lining
Insulin is a protein that would be digested by proteases in the digestive system before reaching the blood
Oral insulin causes dangerous hypoglycaemia
The liver destroys insulin taken orally
questions.answer: Insulin is a protein that would be digested by proteases in the digestive system before reaching the blood
questions.explanation: Insulin is a protein hormone. If taken orally, proteases (pepsin in the stomach, trypsin and chymotrypsin in the small intestine) would break it down into amino acids, destroying its hormonal activity long before it could reach the bloodstream. Injection delivers insulin directly into subcutaneous tissue, from where it diffuses into blood.

content.body: Osmoregulation is the regulation of water potential and ion concentration in body fluids. In humans, the kidneys are the primary organs of osmoregulation and excretion. They filter blood, remove metabolic waste products (especially urea), and regulate blood water potential.
Structure of the Kidney and Nephron

items: Each kidney contains approximately 1 million nephrons — the functional filtering units.
Bowman's capsule: cup-shaped structure surrounding the glomerulus. Filtrate from the blood collects here.
Glomerulus: a knot of capillaries inside Bowman's capsule. High blood pressure forces small molecules out of blood into Bowman's capsule — ultrafiltration.
Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT): reabsorbs glucose (all), amino acids (all), water, Na⁺, Cl⁻, K⁺ — by active transport and osmosis.

Loop of Henle: creates an osmotic gradient in the medulla — essential for producing concentrated urine. Descending limb: permeable to water (water leaves by osmosis). Ascending limb: impermeable to water, actively transports Na⁺ and Cl⁻ out. This countercurrent multiplier mechanism concentrates the medullary tissue.
Distal convoluted tubule (DCT): fine-tuning of ion reabsorption (regulated by aldosterone).
Collecting duct: final water reabsorption — regulated by ADH (antidiuretic hormone). Passes through the concentrated medulla — if ADH makes the duct permeable to water, water is drawn out by osmosis.

content.body: ADH (antidiuretic hormone, also called vasopressin) is produced in the hypothalamus and released from the posterior pituitary gland. It controls the permeability of the collecting duct to water.

content.heading: When blood water potential falls (body is dehydrated — e.g. after exercise, sweating, not drinking):
items: Osmoreceptors in the hypothalamus detect the fall in blood water potential.
Hypothalamus signals the posterior pituitary to release more ADH.
ADH travels in the blood to the collecting duct.
ADH binds to receptors on collecting duct cells → triggers insertion of aquaporin water channels into the cell membrane.
More aquaporins = more water reabsorbed from the filtrate back into the blood by osmosis.
Result: small volume of concentrated (dark yellow) urine is produced.
Blood water potential returns to normal → ADH secretion decreases (negative feedback).

content.heading: When blood water potential rises (after drinking a lot of water):
items: Osmoreceptors detect rise in blood water potential.
Less ADH released from posterior pituitary.
Fewer aquaporins in collecting duct → less water reabsorbed.
Result: large volume of dilute (pale/colourless) urine produced.
Blood water potential returns to normal (negative feedback).

content.heading: Xerophytes (adapted to dry conditions — e.g. acacia, cactus, sisal):
items: Thick waxy cuticle: waterproof — reduces transpiration from leaf surface.
Sunken stomata: stomata in pits below leaf surface — reduces air movement over them, reducing water loss by diffusion.
Rolled leaves: rolling reduces surface area exposed to dry air — reduces transpiration.
Reduced leaf surface area: some have leaves reduced to spines (cacti) — minimises transpiration.
Deep/extensive roots: tap roots reach deep water tables; shallow extensive roots capture surface water after rain.

CAM photosynthesis (in some xerophytes): stomata open at night to absorb CO₂, which is stored as malic acid. During the day, stomata close — CO₂ is released from malic acid for photosynthesis. Prevents water loss while allowing CO₂ uptake.
Succulent tissues: store water in thick stems (e.g. cactus) or leaves (e.g. aloe vera).

content.heading: Hydrophytes (adapted to aquatic/waterlogged conditions — e.g. water lily, papyrus):
items: Stomata on upper leaf surface only (or both surfaces) — as leaves float on water, lower surface is not exposed to air.
Large air spaces (aerenchyma) in stems and leaves: provide buoyancy and allow gas diffusion to submerged parts.
Thin or absent waxy cuticle: no need to prevent water loss.
Flexible stems: bend with water currents without breaking.
Poorly developed roots: dissolved minerals are absorbed directly from water through leaves.

content.heading: Mesophytes (moderate water conditions — most Uganda crops: maize, beans, tomatoes):
items: Broad leaves with thin cuticle.
Stomata on both leaf surfaces (more on lower surface — abaxial).
Well-developed root system.
No extreme adaptations.
Latex: milky fluid secreted into laticifer vessels — contains rubber, terpenes, alkaloids. Found in rubber tree, euphorbia, papaya. Commercial use: natural rubber production.

Anthocyanins: water-soluble pigments (red, purple, blue) — may be waste products of flavonoid metabolism. Give colour to purple flowers, red cabbage, anthocyanin-rich fruits. Antioxidant applications in food industry.
Essential oils/resins: terpenes secreted into resin ducts (pine trees) or oil glands (eucalyptus leaves). Antimicrobial, herbivore deterrent. Commercial uses: perfumes, medicines, incense.
Quinine: alkaloid in bark of cinchona tree. Was the first effective treatment for malaria. Still used in some antimalarial drugs and tonic water.
Saponins: soapy compounds in many plants (e.g. soapwort, soya beans). Act as natural detergents. Applications: pharmaceuticals, food industry, natural soap.

questions.question: A person drinks very little water all day in Kampala's heat. What happens to their ADH levels and urine?
options: ADH increases → dilute, high volume urine
ADH decreases → concentrated, low volume urine
ADH increases → concentrated, low volume urine
ADH decreases → dilute, high volume urine
questions.answer: ADH increases → concentrated, low volume urine
questions.explanation: Dehydration reduces blood water potential. Osmoreceptors detect this and trigger more ADH release. ADH inserts aquaporins into collecting duct, increasing water reabsorption. Result: small volume of dark, concentrated urine — conserving body water.

questions.question: Ultrafiltration in the Bowman's capsule filters molecules based on size. Which substance would NOT normally be found in the filtrate?
options: Glucose
Urea
Plasma proteins (e.g. albumin)
Water
questions.explanation: The glomerular filtration membrane has pores too small for large plasma proteins. Glucose, urea, water, ions, and amino acids all pass through. Plasma proteins remain in the blood. If proteins appear in urine (proteinuria), it indicates glomerular membrane damage — e.g. in diabetes or kidney disease.

questions.question: An acacia tree on the dry Karamoja plains has very small leaves covered in a thick waxy cuticle and deep tap roots. These adaptations are characteristic of:
options: A hydrophyte
A mesophyte
A xerophyte
A CAM plant only
questions.explanation: Xerophytes are adapted to water-scarce environments. Small leaves (reduced surface area for transpiration), thick waxy cuticle (reduces cuticular transpiration), and deep roots (access groundwater) are all xerophytic adaptations that reduce water loss and maximise water uptake in dry conditions.

questions.question: Quinine, extracted from the bark of the cinchona tree, was historically important in Uganda because:
options: It was used as a rubber substitute
It was the first effective treatment for malaria
It produces a soapy lather for cleaning
It is used as a natural food dye
questions.answer: It was the first effective treatment for malaria
questions.explanation: Quinine is an alkaloid that interferes with the malaria parasite's ability to break down haemoglobin. It was the first effective antimalarial drug, historically critical in Uganda and across tropical Africa. It is still used today in some cases and as an ingredient in tonic water.
